name: Layered Low Resistivity — Aquifer
description: Continuous subhorizontal low-resistivity layer consistent with saturated zone or aquifer.

conclusion.hypothesisStatement: Subhorizontal low-resistivity zone is consistent with a {{entity}}. Resistivity values and continuity suggest saturated granular or fractured material.
additionalRecommendations: Verify resistivity range — freshwater aquifer: 20–100 Ohm.m; saline: <10 Ohm.m
Compare with borehole logs if available
IP survey may distinguish clay from water saturation

name: Elongated High Resistivity — Palaeochannel
description: Elongated resistive body within lower-resistivity host — likely dry gravel-filled palaeochannel.

conclusion.hypothesisStatement: Elongated resistive body is consistent with a {{entity}} — dry coarse-grained fill (gravel/sand) commonly produces 300–2000 Ohm.m contrast against finer-grained host.
additionalRecommendations: Extend profile along channel axis to map continuity
Drill to confirm grain size and saturation state

name: Vertical Conductive Zone — Fault or Shear
description: Near-vertical low-resistivity zone cutting through host — fault gouge, graphite, or sulphide shear zone.

conclusion.hypothesisStatement: Sub-vertical conductive zone with sharp lateral gradients is consistent with a {{entity}} or fault zone. Graphitic, clay-rich, or sulphide-bearing shear fabric are common causes.
additionalRecommendations: IP chargeability survey to distinguish graphite (non-chargeable) from sulphide (chargeable)

name: Circular Resistive Body — Intrusion
description: Circular high-resistivity anomaly — massive crystalline rock, silicified zone, or unaltered intrusion.

conclusion.hypothesisStatement: Circular resistive anomaly is consistent with a massive {{entity}} or silicified zone. Massive unaltered crystalline rocks commonly have resistivity >1000 Ohm.m.
additionalRecommendations: Coincident magnetic data recommended to confirm lithological interpretation
Inversion required to constrain geometry at depth

name: Surface Conductive Layer — Regolith
description: Thin surface conductive layer overlying resistive basement — typical weathered regolith profile.

conclusion.hypothesisStatement: Near-surface conductive layer is consistent with a clay-rich {{entity}}. This is a commonly observed two-layer response: conductive regolith over resistive fresh basement.
additionalRecommendations: Estimate regolith thickness from inversion depth-to-basement
Account for static shift effects if surface resistivity is very low

name: Inversion Non-Uniqueness Warning
description: ERT inversion has inherent equivalence — multiple models may fit the same data.

conclusion.hypothesisStatement: Processing assumption: ERT inversion is non-unique. Thin conductive layers and thick less-conductive layers may produce equivalent responses. Depth estimates carry inherent uncertainty of ±15–30%.
additionalRecommendations: Use depth-of-investigation (DOI) index to flag poorly constrained regions

name: Suppression Effect Warning
description: Overlying conductive layer may suppress response from deeper resistive features.

conclusion.hypothesisStatement: Uncertainty warning: a conductive surface layer may suppress detection of deeper resistive targets. Features below the conductor may be underestimated or invisible to the survey.
additionalRecommendations: Consider TEM or CSAMT for greater depth penetration through conductive cover
Increase electrode spacing to improve depth sensitivity

name: Alteration Zone — Variable Resistivity
description: Irregular resistivity variation with distinct zones — consistent with hydrothermal alteration zoning.

conclusion.hypothesisStatement: Irregular resistivity pattern with resistive core and conductive halo is consistent with a {{entity}} — silicic core is resistive; argillic alteration halo is conductive.
additionalRecommendations: IP survey critical — sulphide mineralisation in alteration zone produces chargeability anomaly
